item.title: Short but to the point
item.content:encoded: I enjoyed this oldie with a few differences it could have ended up a sarkism scp.

item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
The first image originates from epa.gov. As such, it is released into the public domain.

The second image could not be sourced reliably and is assumed not compliant with CC 3.0. As such, it has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A mind affecting mold anomaly. This is the simple- and good- kind of articles I expect to see from series 1.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Given the so standard "it makes people spread itself" changes in its hosts, I'd gather its a secondary cerebral manipulation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That would depend on whether the effect is to the brain or the eyeball or, put another way, can the eye not see it or can the brain not recognise that it is being seen. If the latter then yes, it's an antimemetic effect. If the former then it's a physical effect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Samples of SCP-020 exhibit an unknown effect that renders them effectively invisible to direct observation, even when under a microscope. SCP-020 is only visible to humans when viewed through photographic or video surveillance.

Would this be considered a (weak) Antimemetic effect? Or is this more akin to the SCP-2480 invisibility effect?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That repurposed psoriasis pic looks really cheesy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Last line ruined the tone. "Gunfire , screaming" . Gunfire, screaming, death, running running running , . Made me laugh. Otherwise good write up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I know this article's thread is dead, but I was making a YouTube thumbnail for TheHauntedReader about it just now and re-read it. When I did, I noticed:

SCP-020 was first encountered in [REDACTED], where an undercover SCP agent noted dramatic personality changes in personnel working at the local hospital.

It's pretty standard now to call them Foundation agents, given that that's the actual informal title of the organization and SCP is just an acronym for Special Containment Procedures and/or Secure Contain Protect. At least, it's always corrected in new articles posted these days, and I think I've seen it changed in older ones to fit current site standards.

item.title: Truly disgusting
item.content:encoded: Ugh. This makes me shiver. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Given my allergy to mold spores, this one creeps me the fuck out.

That said, a few posts up mentioned our social media society. A slight tweak indicating it was contained in 198X might be indicated.

item.title: STAFF POST - CLOSED
item.content:encoded:
This is like the third one of these you've posted now. "Not really feeling this one" does nothing to help anyone improve anything (yes, this even applies to ancient Series I articles), and is directly in violation of Rule 2 of the Criticism Policy.

It's fine if you don't have anything to say aside from your vote. But if you've got nothing to say, don't bother posting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not really feeling this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Horrible. Just disgusting and horrible. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Creeped right the heck out and back by the whole "We just wanted some guests." thing. "We", huh? Followed up by the lady saying something that had to be removed, and that we get no indication of what it was just ramps up the interest in what else exactly is going on with this thing.

Can't really claim to be impressed with the town being wiped out, though. The Foundation was portrayed as being very kill happy in these earlier articles, to the point where this doesn't even seem like that big a deal for them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't find this idea to be too original, which isn't my main issue with it. The problem that I have is that it is sort of dull. Nothing interesting happens and as someone mensioned it isn't creepy either.

It shows the lengths that the Foundation will go to contain the SCPs.
I mean sure, 110-Montauk is horrible, but it's still centralized. In this instance, they
wiped out an ENTIRE TOWN.

I agree with ProfessorBlackJack, this was probably my favourite thing in this SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Was the 100th upvote. Yay.

Obviously this is an oldie, so it doesn't have quite the same standards as we have now, but even so, it's very good for its time. I know there a few higher-ups that say it's uninteresting, but I find the idea of spores and fungus infections and such that can do creepy shit like, oh I don't know, turn people into zombies (even though this specific thing doesn't do that) extremely interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The point of how recently it was contained is a fair one - another way to resolve my mental disconnect would just be to add mention of it being contained in 19██. As to affected people not being social, part of the horror of the fungus is the woman inviting the team inside, and the implication that if anything, the fungus makes those affected more social.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You assume that it was contained recently.

And that affected people would be social.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ok, I have to downvote for now. I like the notion of the invisible fungus that's only visible under specific conditions, but in our Facebook/twitter/imgur-centric society, people are getting pictures taken of themselves all the time. There's no way for instances of this to escape notice unless they're a) only visible to specific cameras (say UV or IR) or are only showing up in populations much less likely to be photographed - say retirement homes.

As a side note, this detail was what finally gave me the impetus to join the foundation to be able to comment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The idea of someone visiting their family and taking some photos while in their house, and then seeing this thing covering up the entire place - and the residents! - is really creep.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this one. It shows the lengths that the Foundation will go to contain the SCPs.
I mean sure, 110-Montauk is horrible, but it's still centralized. In this instance, they wiped out an ENTIRE TOWN.
Also, the redacted comment that the infected woman said really upped the creep factor for me. What could she have said that would require the brass to remove it? That adds a whole new layer to the horror.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it just as it is. The idea that there's something that could be growing in your house or on you without you even noticing it or being able to see it is pretty freaky. This one actually makes me want to go taking pictures of everything just to make sure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
(since I'm reading through all the contagion tags)

… this could be a lot creepier, especially because the pictures are pretty good. But there's a high bar set for mind-control fungus in fiction, and all I really thought after reading was "Mushishi did this so much better". I mean, the wrong dose of meds can make you aggressive. That's a very familiar human concept.

The idea of a living thing using you as limbs or eyes or warmth or nutrient source is a lot creepier, and deserves to be explored further.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Like that sufferers can't see it. Creepy. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The new name is appreciated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The concept definitely has some serious creep factor. I do agree that the writeup could use some work, but I'm drawing a blank as to what would help.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Kinda underplayed, but I'm still pretty freaked out by the idea that weird stuff is growing on me and I don't know it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not very interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconded. I like where it starts, but it stops before any useful or colorful description can begin.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Huh… I like the idea, but you should try to amp up the creepy- like, it's almost underplayed here (some would argue this is the point, some are wrong, keep reading). Maybe, quotes of affected behavior, or, how the affected people start doing even weirder and creepier things… Hints at some sort of related entity or sentience of the fungus could be fun too… Look, go to town with it (within limits).
